original_hypothesis.title: Predictable On-Chain Randomness in NextGenRandomizerNXT Allows Trait Sniping
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenRandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash uses keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_mintIndex, blockhash(block.number - 1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord())). Both randomNumber() and randomWord() in XRandoms use block.prevrandao, blockhash(block.number-1), and block.timestamp - all known to the caller at execution time. A minter using a smart contract can predict the exact token hash before committing to the mint, selectively minting only when rare traits would be generated. The _saltfun_o parameter is unused in the hash computation, providing no additional entropy.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: All inputs to the hash function are deterministic at execution time: mintIndex is calculable from viewCirSupply, block variables are known to the executing contract, and XRandoms provides no independent entropy. This enables selective minting for trait manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_mintIndex, blockhash(block.number-1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord())); all inputs deterministic at execution time

preconditions: Collection uses NextGenRandomizerNXT as its randomizer (not VRF or ARRNG)
Token traits/rarity derived from tokenToHash are economically valuable
Attacker deploys contract that computes hash before committing to mint

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All hash inputs are block-level variables known to any executing contract; no commit-reveal scheme; _saltfun_o unused
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any mint on collections using NextGenRandomizerNXT

operational_questions: Which collections use NextGenRandomizerNXT vs VRF/ARRNG?
How are token traits derived from tokenToHash?
What is the economic value differential between common and rare traits?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract that pre-computes hash and selectively mints
Verify hash prediction matches actual token hash
Test with actual generative art scripts to confirm trait selection

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy contract that computes the would-be token hash using current block variables
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Contract checks if predicted hash produces desirable traits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If desirable, proceed with mint; if not, revert to save gas (minus base cost)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat across blocks until rare traits are captured

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Rare traits systematically captured by attackers; fair distribution destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Token hashes immutable once set; cannot retroactively fix trait distribution

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs understanding of hash-to-trait mapping
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any active mint period for NXT-randomized collections

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract-based minters with high rare-trait frequency
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Statistical anomalies in trait distribution by minter type
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Minting contracts that frequently revert then succeed

validation_result.impact_summary: All inputs to NextGenRandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash are deterministic within the executing block, allowing a smart contract minter to pre-compute the token hash and selectively mint only when desirable/rare traits would result.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis is correct in all material aspects. NextGenRandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash computes: keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_mintIndex, blockhash(block.number - 1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord())). Every input is deterministic at execution time within the same transaction/block:

1. _mintIndex: Publicly calculable as viewTokensIndexMin(col) + viewCirSupply(col).
2. blockhash(block.number - 1): Known to any contract executing in the same block.
3. randoms.randomNumber(): Uses block.prevrandao, blockhash(block.number-1), block.timestamp — all known within the block.
4. randoms.randomWord(): Uses identical block variables as randomNumber(), then maps to a word via getWord().

Since calculateTokenHash is called synchronously during the mint transaction (via _mintProcessing in NextGenCore), an attacker contract can replicate the exact hash computation before committing to the mint. If the hash would produce undesirable traits, the attacker reverts the transaction. The _saltfun_o parameter is accepted but completely unused in the hash computation, providing zero additional entropy.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code inspection confirms all claims in the hypothesis. The hash function at RandomizerNXT.sol:57 uses only block-level variables and a deterministic mint index. XRandoms.sol randomNumber() and randomWord() at lines 36-43 both use exclusively block.prevrandao, blockhash(block.number-1), and block.timestamp. The _saltfun_o parameter appears in the function signature at RandomizerNXT.sol:55 but is never referenced in the hash computation at line 57.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: All hash inputs are deterministic within the executing block. _saltfun_o is accepted as parameter but never used. An attacker contract can pre-compute the exact hash before the mint executes.
Uses only block-level variables (block.prevrandao, blockhash, block.timestamp) which are all known to any contract executing in the same block. Provides zero independent entropy.
Uses the exact same block-level variables as randomNumber(). Both functions called from the same block return deterministic values, adding no additional entropy to the hash.

calling_sequence: 1. User/attacker contract calls MinterContract.mint() with payment
5. NextGenRandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash() computes hash from block variables at RandomizerNXT.sol:57
6. Hash is set via gencoreContract.setTokenHash() at RandomizerNXT.sol:58
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire flow from mint to hash computation executes in a single transaction. At step 5, all inputs to keccak256 are known: _mintIndex is deterministic (viewTokensIndexMin + viewCirSupply), blockhash(block.number-1) is the same for all transactions in the block, and randoms.randomNumber()/randomWord() use only block.prevrandao, blockhash(block.number-1), and block.timestamp — all deterministic within the block. An attacker contract can replicate this computation before calling mint(), and revert if the hash doesn't produce desired traits.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Collection uses NextGenRandomizerNXT as its randomizer
config_validation.deployment_evidence: fixturesDeployment.js deploys NextGenRandomizerNXT as the randomizer (line: const randomizer = await ethers.getContractFactory('NextGenRandomizerNXT')), confirming NXT randomizer is a supported and default configuration in the test deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Attack is straightforward and realistic:
1. Attacker deploys a contract that: (a) reads current block variables and viewCirSupply to compute _mintIndex, (b) replicates the keccak256 computation from RandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash using the same block variables, (c) evaluates whether the resulting hash produces desirable traits based on the collection's generative art logic, (d) calls MinterContract.mint() only if traits are desirable, otherwise reverts.
2. The attacker repeats across blocks until rare traits are captured.
3. Cost of failed attempts is only base transaction gas (reverts before state changes complete).
4. No coordination or special privileges needed — any minter can execute this during public or allowlist phases.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact on fairness and economic integrity of any collection using NextGenRandomizerNXT. Rare traits that should be randomly distributed can be systematically captured by attacker contracts, destroying the fair distribution guarantee. Token hashes are immutable once set (enforced by setTokenHash's zero-check), so affected tokens cannot be retroactively fixed. Collections using VRF or ARRNG randomizers are not affected, as those provide async off-chain randomness.
recommended_mitigations: Use Chainlink VRF (RandomizerVRF) or ARRNG (RandomizerRNG) instead of NXT for production deployments, as they provide asynchronous off-chain randomness that cannot be predicted
If on-chain randomness must be used, implement a commit-reveal scheme where the hash is computed in a separate transaction from the mint
Include _saltfun_o in the hash computation to at least add admin-controlled entropy
Add a check to prevent smart contracts from calling mint() (e.g., require(tx.origin == msg.sender)) — though this has its own drawbacks and doesn't fully mitigate the issue for validators
Consider using block.number + N future blockhash with a separate reveal step
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts using fixturesDeployment.js. 2. Create and configure a collection with NXT randomizer. 3. Deploy an attacker contract that: computes the expected hash using the same formula as calculateTokenHash with current block variables, checks if hash meets criteria, then calls mint() or reverts. 4. Call the attacker contract and verify the predicted hash matches the actual tokenToHash stored on-chain. 5. Demonstrate selective minting by only proceeding when hash meets arbitrary criteria.
uncertainties: The economic impact depends on how token traits/rarity are derived from tokenToHash, which is determined by each collection's generative art script (off-chain logic)

Post-merge, block.prevrandao is provided by the beacon chain and is not manipulable by validators in the same way block.difficulty was by miners, but it IS known to all participants within the same block
additional_testing_needed: Verify with a concrete generative art script that hash prediction translates to trait prediction
Test whether the gas cost of hash pre-computation + conditional revert is economically viable relative to mint cost
Determine which mainnet collections (if any) were deployed with NXT randomizer vs VRF/ARRNG
